questions.question: A 28-year-old woman develops hives, wheezing, and lightheadedness 10 minutes after eating shrimp at a restaurant. Her blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg. According to current guidelines, which clinical criteria best supports the diagnosis of anaphylaxis?
options: A) Presence of urticaria alone requiring antihistamine treatment
B) Involvement of 2+ organ systems after likely allergen exposure: skin (hives), respiratory (wheezing), and cardiovascular (hypotension)
C) Any allergic reaction requiring emergency department evaluation
D) Positive skin prick test to the suspected allergen
questions.explanation: The NIAID/FAAN and WAO criteria define anaphylaxis by clinical presentation, not laboratory testing. Criterion 2 states: likely allergen exposure with involvement of 2 or more organ systems occurring rapidly (skin/mucosal, respiratory, cardiovascular, or GI). This patient has skin involvement (hives), respiratory symptoms (wheezing), and cardiovascular compromise (hypotension) after a likely trigger (shellfish).

questions.question: Which statement about the clinical presentation of anaphylaxis is CORRECT?
options: A) Skin symptoms must always be present to diagnose anaphylaxis
B) Up to 10-20% of patients may present without skin findings
C) GI symptoms are rare and should prompt investigation for other diagnoses
D) Symptoms typically develop over 6-12 hours after allergen exposure
questions.explanation: While cutaneous manifestations occur in 80-90% of cases, anaphylaxis can present WITHOUT skin findings in 10-20% of patients. GI symptoms occur in 25-30% of cases. Symptoms typically develop within minutes to 1-2 hours (except alpha-gal syndrome).

questions.question: According to the 2023 practice parameter, what defines 'severe' anaphylaxis?
options: A) Duration of symptoms greater than 2 hours
B) Presence of cardiovascular collapse or respiratory failure
C) Requirement for emergency department care
D) Involvement of more than 3 organ systems
questions.explanation: Severe anaphylaxis is specifically defined by respiratory failure (hypoxia, severe bronchospasm, stridor) OR cardiovascular collapse (profound hypotension, cardiac arrest, syncope). Life-threatening compromise of critical organ systems determines severity.

questions.question: Which mechanism primarily mediates IgE-dependent anaphylaxis?
options: A) Direct complement activation (C3a/C5a) causing mast cell degranulation
B) IgE crosslinking on FcεRI receptors leading to mast cell and basophil degranulation
C) T-cell mediated cytokine release
D) IgG-dependent activation of neutrophils
questions.explanation: Classic IgE-mediated anaphylaxis occurs when allergen crosslinks IgE antibodies bound to high-affinity FcεRI receptors on mast cells and basophils, triggering degranulation and release of histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes, and PAF.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism by which histamine causes hypotension during anaphylaxis?
options: A) Direct negative inotropic effect on the heart
B) Increased vascular permeability and vasodilation via H1/H2 receptors
C) Stimulation of vagal reflexes causing bradycardia
D) Depletion of intravascular catecholamines
questions.explanation: Histamine acts on H1 and H2 receptors to cause vasodilation and increased vascular permeability, leading to fluid extravasation, tissue edema, and distributive shock through peripheral vasodilation and capillary leak.

questions.question: A patient develops anaphylaxis after receiving radiocontrast for the first time. This reaction is BEST explained by:
options: A) Prior sensitization from environmental exposure
B) Direct mast cell activation via MRGPRX2 receptors (non-IgE mechanism)
C) IgE from a forgotten prior exposure
D) Complement activation requiring prior exposure
questions.explanation: Radiocontrast reactions can occur on first exposure through MRGPRX2 receptors that allow direct mast cell activation without prior sensitization. This receptor responds to radiocontrast, vancomycin, fluoroquinolones, and muscle relaxants.

questions.question: Which statement about anaphylaxis triggers is CORRECT?
options: A) In adults, foods are the most common cause of fatal anaphylaxis
B) In children, medications are the most common trigger overall
C) In adults, medications and stinging insects are the most common triggers
D) Idiopathic anaphylaxis is rare, occurring in less than 1% of cases
questions.explanation: In ADULTS, medications and stinging insects are most common. In CHILDREN, foods and insect stings predominate. Idiopathic anaphylaxis occurs in 15-20% of cases.

questions.question: Which combination represents the HIGHEST risk for fatal anaphylaxis?
options: A) Young child with milk allergy and no asthma
B) Adult with bee sting allergy taking an SSRI
C) Adolescent with peanut allergy, poorly controlled asthma, and delayed epinephrine
D) Elderly patient with shellfish allergy and diabetes
questions.explanation: Major risk factors: poorly controlled asthma, peanut/tree nut allergies, adolescents/young adults, delayed epinephrine. This patient has THREE major risk factors. Delayed epinephrine is associated with 70% of fatal food reactions.

questions.question: A man experiences severe anaphylaxis 4-6 hours after eating a hamburger, with recent tick exposure history. The MOST likely diagnosis is:
options: A) Standard food allergy to beef protein
B) Alpha-gal syndrome (galactose-alpha-1,3-galactose allergy)
C) Bacterial food poisoning
D) Idiopathic anaphylaxis
questions.explanation: Alpha-gal syndrome is IgE-mediated allergy to galactose-alpha-1,3-galactose in mammalian meat, developing after lone star tick bites. Reactions characteristically occur 3-6 hours after eating red meat.

questions.question: What is the recommended first-line dose and route of epinephrine for an adult with anaphylaxis?
options: A) 0.1 mg IV push as the preferred route
B) 0.3-0.5 mg intramuscularly in the anterolateral thigh
C) 0.5 mg subcutaneously in the deltoid
D) 1 mg nebulized for respiratory symptoms
questions.explanation: First-line treatment is epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM in the anterolateral thigh. IM thigh injection achieves peak levels in ~8 minutes vs ~34 minutes for subcutaneous. IV is reserved for refractory cases.

questions.question: A 10-year-old child weighing 25 kg is having anaphylaxis. What is the appropriate epinephrine autoinjector dose?
options: A) 0.1 mg autoinjector
B) 0.15 mg autoinjector (EpiPen Jr)
C) 0.3 mg autoinjector (EpiPen)
D) Half of the 0.3 mg autoinjector
questions.explanation: Pediatric dosing is 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.3 mg). For 15-30 kg children, the 0.15 mg autoinjector is recommended. The 0.3 mg autoinjector is for children >30 kg.

questions.question: A patient on a beta-blocker develops anaphylaxis refractory to 2 doses of IM epinephrine. What is the MOST appropriate next medication?
options: A) High-dose IV corticosteroids
B) IV diphenhydramine
C) IV glucagon
D) IV norepinephrine
questions.explanation: Glucagon is the drug of choice for refractory anaphylaxis in beta-blocked patients. It activates adenylate cyclase independently of beta-receptors. Dose: 1-5 mg IV over 5 minutes.

questions.question: What is 'refractory anaphylaxis' and how is it treated?
options: A) Not responding to antihistamines; treat with IV corticosteroids
B) Ongoing symptoms despite 2 doses of IM epinephrine; initiate IV epinephrine infusion
C) Recurring after 1 hour; repeat IM epinephrine
D) In a patient allergic to epinephrine; use IV vasopressin
questions.explanation: Refractory anaphylaxis is ongoing symptoms despite 2 appropriate IM epinephrine doses (~1% of severe cases). Treatment: IV epinephrine infusion at 5-20 mcg/min (1 mg in 1000 mL = 1 mcg/mL).

sources.title: Deep Dive into Evidence: Anaphylaxis - EMRA

questions.question: What is the current evidence regarding antihistamines in acute anaphylaxis?
options: A) H1-blockers prevent airway obstruction and should be given before epinephrine
B) H1 and H2 blockers have NOT been shown to reverse upper airway obstruction or hypotension
C) IV diphenhydramine is equally effective as epinephrine
D) Antihistamines should replace epinephrine in mild anaphylaxis
questions.explanation: Antihistamines have NOT been shown to reverse upper airway obstruction or hypotension. They may relieve cutaneous symptoms but are ADJUNCTIVE therapy AFTER epinephrine. Recent guidelines recommend AGAINST routine use.

sources.title: Deep Dive into Evidence: Anaphylaxis - EMRA

questions.question: What is the evidence for corticosteroid use in acute anaphylaxis?
options: A) Strong evidence supports corticosteroids to prevent biphasic reactions
B) Corticosteroids have rapid onset helping with initial symptoms
C) Corticosteroids have ~6 hour onset and have NOT been proven to prevent biphasic reactions
D) Corticosteroids are contraindicated in anaphylaxis
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids have ~4-6 hour onset with NO effect on acute phase. Systematic reviews found INSUFFICIENT evidence they prevent biphasic reactions. Recent guidelines recommend AGAINST routine use.

sources.title: Deep Dive into Evidence: Anaphylaxis - EMRA

questions.question: Which adjunctive therapy is appropriate for persistent bronchospasm despite epinephrine?
options: A) Inhaled ipratropium only
B) Inhaled beta-2 agonist (albuterol)
C) IV aminophylline
D) High-dose IV magnesium sulfate
questions.explanation: Inhaled beta-2 agonists provide additional bronchodilation for persistent bronchospasm, especially important in asthmatic patients. Standard dosing: 2.5-5 mg nebulized.

questions.question: What is a biphasic anaphylactic reaction and how often does it occur?
options: A) Recurrence after re-exposure; occurs in 50% of cases
B) Recurrence without re-exposure within 1-72 hours; occurs in <5% of cases
C) Progressive worsening of initial symptoms; occurs in 25%
D) Development of new allergies; occurs in 10%
questions.explanation: Biphasic reactions are recurrence of symptoms WITHOUT re-exposure, typically within 1-72 hours (most 8-12 hours). They occur in <5% of cases (range 1-5%) and can rarely be fatal.

sources.title: Time of Onset and Predictors of Biphasic Reactions - PubMed

questions.question: What is the recommended observation period after anaphylaxis?
options: A) All patients should be observed for 24 hours
B) Minimum 4-6 hours; longer (8-12+ hours) for severe reactions or high-risk features
C) 2 hours is sufficient for all patients
D) No observation needed if symptoms resolve after one epinephrine dose
questions.explanation: ≥6 hours observation excludes recurrence in >95% of patients. Minimum 4-6 hours for moderate reactions; 8-12+ hours for severe presentations, multiple epinephrine doses, or risk factors.

sources.title: Duration of Observation for Biphasic Reaction - PubMed

questions.question: Which factors are associated with INCREASED risk of biphasic anaphylaxis?
options: A) Food triggers and rapid response to first epinephrine dose
B) Unknown trigger, severe initial reaction, hypotension, and need for multiple epinephrine doses
C) Young age and skin-only manifestations
D) Prior history of mild allergic reactions only
questions.explanation: Risk factors: unknown trigger, severe initial reaction, hypotension, laryngeal edema, delayed epinephrine, multiple epinephrine doses needed. FOOD triggers are actually associated with LOWER biphasic risk.

sources.title: Time of Onset and Predictors of Biphasic Reactions - PubMed
Risk Factors for Biphasic Anaphylaxis - ScienceDirect

layers.layer_title: Serum Tryptase and Mast Cell Considerations

questions.question: When is serum tryptase measurement MOST useful in anaphylaxis evaluation?
options: A) As a screening test before allergen exposure
B) To confirm mast cell activation when clinical diagnosis is uncertain
C) To determine the specific allergen trigger
D) To predict severity of future reactions
questions.explanation: Tryptase confirms mast cell degranulation when diagnosis is uncertain. Draw 15-60 min to 3 hours after onset. Elevation >20% + 2 ng/mL above baseline supports diagnosis. Sensitivity is only 30-50%; normal tryptase does NOT exclude anaphylaxis.

questions.question: A patient with recurrent severe bee sting anaphylaxis has baseline tryptase of 15.2 ng/mL. What is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: A) Reassurance that elevated tryptase is common and benign
B) Evaluation for mastocytosis including bone marrow biopsy consideration
C) Repeat tryptase in 1 week
D) Start prophylactic antihistamines only
questions.explanation: Elevated baseline tryptase (>11.4 ng/mL) with severe Hymenoptera anaphylaxis warrants evaluation for mastocytosis. Bone marrow biopsy should be considered. Also screen for hereditary alpha-tryptasemia (KIT D816V mutation + tryptase genotyping).

questions.question: What is hereditary alpha-tryptasemia (HαT) and its significance in anaphylaxis?
options: A) A rare autosomal recessive disorder causing mast cell tumors
B) An autosomal dominant trait with increased TPSAB1 copies; 4-6% of population; increased anaphylaxis risk
C) An acquired condition from repeated allergen exposures
D) A benign finding with no clinical significance
questions.explanation: HαT is autosomal dominant, caused by increased TPSAB1 gene copies encoding alpha-tryptase. Affects 4-6% of population (most common cause of elevated baseline tryptase). Associated with 2-3 fold increased severe anaphylaxis risk, especially to insect stings.

questions.question: Following an anaphylactic reaction, what is the MOST important discharge component?
options: A) Prescription for oral corticosteroids
B) Epinephrine autoinjector prescription with training and allergist referral
C) Antihistamine prescription for first sign of symptoms
D) Reassurance that reactions don't recur
questions.explanation: Critical discharge components: 1) Epinephrine autoinjector (2 devices), 2) Hands-on training, 3) Written action plan, 4) Allergist referral, 5) Trigger avoidance education. Counsel to use epinephrine at FIRST sign of anaphylaxis.

questions.question: A patient with severe bee sting anaphylaxis takes lisinopril for hypertension. What is the guideline recommendation regarding ACE inhibitor use?
options: A) ACE inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated
B) Stop ACE inhibitors 48 hours before outdoor activities
C) For most patients, the risk of changing ACE inhibitors may exceed the risk of more severe anaphylaxis
D) ACE inhibitors protect against anaphylaxis
questions.explanation: The 2023 parameter states that for most indications, the risk of stopping beta-blockers or ACE inhibitors may EXCEED the risk of more severe anaphylaxis, especially in patients who can receive venom immunotherapy (97-98% effective). Shared decision-making with cardiology is recommended.

clinical_pearls: Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment - antihistamines and corticosteroids should NEVER delay epinephrine
IM epinephrine (0.3-0.5 mg adults) in anterolateral thigh achieves faster peak levels than subcutaneous or deltoid
10-20% of anaphylaxis cases may present WITHOUT skin findings - don't exclude based on absent urticaria
Biphasic reactions occur in <5% of cases; observe minimum 4-6 hours, longer for severe or high-risk cases
Elevated baseline tryptase (>11.4 ng/mL) warrants evaluation for mastocytosis and hereditary alpha-tryptasemia
Glucagon is the drug of choice for refractory anaphylaxis in patients on beta-blockers
Normal tryptase does NOT exclude anaphylaxis - sensitivity is only 30-50%
Alpha-gal syndrome presents 3-6 hours after mammalian meat - suspect with delayed anaphylaxis and tick exposure
All patients need epinephrine autoinjector prescription, training, action plan, and allergist referral

references.title: Deep Dive into Evidence: Anaphylaxis - EMRA
